policy_statement: Strengthen inclusion of non-governmental actors in planning and implementing adaptation measures during the NDC implementation period (A6).
mechanism_description: Strengthening the inclusion of non-governmental actors in planning and implementing adaptation measures improves coordination and delivery capacity, supporting overall adaptive capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance capacity building → ↑ coordination/implementation of adaptation actions → ↓ vulnerability & climate-impact damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: Adaptation planning and delivery processes are restructured to systematically include non-governmental actors (e.g., communities, NGOs, academia, private sector) in decision-making, implementation, and monitoring.  
(2) Mediating process: Broader participation improves information quality (local risk knowledge), legitimacy and buy-in, inter-organizational coordination, resource mobilization, and accountability—raising institutional adaptive capacity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: More timely, equitable, and effective adaptation measures are selected and executed, reducing exposure and sensitivity to climate hazards and lowering climate-related losses.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved adaptive capacity that reduces climate-risk impacts by enabling better-designed and better-implemented adaptation actions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance/process change → stronger institutional capacity → improved adaptation implementation → reduced climate vulnerability/impacts.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The mechanism targets managing climate hazards and impacts (capacity to adapt), not primarily reducing GHG emissions (Mitigation), reducing resource inputs for the same service (Resource Efficiency), or enhancing ecosystems as the main mediator (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Stakeholder inclusion can sometimes accelerate mitigation (e.g., consensus for decarbonization), but in this canonical mechanism it is explicitly tied to adaptation planning and “adaptive capacity,” making the dominant effect adaptation.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy whose mechanism is “stakeholder inclusion in adaptation planning → institutional adaptive capacity” primarily operates through reducing climate risk via improved adaptive governance, so Adaptation is consistently the primary category.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype (e.g., governance & capacity, community resilience, planning/monitoring) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assignment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a stakeholder-inclusion policy is framed as general climate planning (not specifically adaptation) or is used mainly to unlock emissions-reduction measures, the dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation; this canonical mechanism is adaptation-specific, so classification remains Adaptation.
key_indicators: “inclusion of non-governmental actors”; “planning and implementation of adaptation measures”; “will be strengthened”; “During the implementation period of this NDC”
co_benefits: Improved governance/participation and social inclusion; potential development benefits from stronger civil-society/private-sector engagement
instance_edge_case_notes: Text does not specify the means of strengthening inclusion (e.g., mandate vs. council creation vs. funding), so classification is at the strategy/approach level rather than a more specific instrument type.
